ecosystem: river (spawning migration, holding), open ocean (growth phase)
season: winter (Nov-Apr primary Puget Sound and coastal WA)

taxonomy.species: O. mykiss (same species as Rainbow Trout)
taxonomy.note: Species 3 in the Salmonidae chapter. Anadromous form of O. mykiss = Steelhead; resident form = Rainbow Trout.

biology.size: 2-5 kg typical; exceptional fish to 8-12+ kg
biology.life_history: Anadromous: born in river, smoltify 1-3 years, ocean 1-4 years, return to spawn
biology.iteroparity: Can spawn multiple times (unlike semelparous Pacific salmon). Kelts (post-spawn fish) may return to ocean and spawn again.
biology.coloration: Ocean phase: metallic steel-grey dorsal, bright silver sides. Spawning: pink lateral stripe returns.
run_types: Winter Steelhead (Nov-Apr entry, Feb-May spawn): primary Puget Sound and coastal WA run
Summer Steelhead (May-Oct entry, spring spawn): Columbia River tributaries primarily

sound_profile.description: Metallic, bright, clean jump. Higher frequency dominant than Chinook body-slap. Slender body enters water cleanly (steeper angle, less lateral splash). Brief duration -- a crack, then quiet.
sound_profile.energy_note: E=5 (below Chinook/Coho E=6): lower mass + cleaner entry = less acoustic energy. Episodic, not continuous.
sound_profile.acrobatics: Steelhead leap more frequently than other Pacific salmon; also leap during angling fights

conservation.olympic_peninsula_crisis: Wild winter Steelhead critically declining on Hoh, Sol Duc, Quillayute, Bogachiel rivers. WDFW emergency closures issued. Most urgent steelhead conservation crisis in Washington.
conservation.hatchery_issue: Hatchery Steelhead interbreeding with wild fish reduces genetic diversity. Management tension between fishery access and wild conservation.

theory_nodes.concept: Steelhead = O. mykiss anadromous form. Same species as Rainbow Trout. The anadromous life history (smoltification, ocean growth, natal return) is the difference. Iteroparous unlike semelparous Pacific salmon -- may spawn multiple times.
theory_nodes.description: The biological relationship between Steelhead and Rainbow Trout.

theory_nodes.concept: Metallic clean jump E=5: slender body (higher length/mass ratio than Chinook) enters water cleanly. Higher-frequency dominant, shorter duration than Chinook E=6 body-slap. Acoustic texture is 'bright and brief' not 'heavy and sustained'. E=5 correct.
theory_nodes.description: Why the Steelhead leap is E=5 and acoustically distinct from Chinook.

theory_nodes.concept: Olympic Peninsula wild winter Steelhead crisis: WDFW emergency closures on Hoh, Sol Duc, others. Critical decline of wild fish populations despite habitat quality. Complex of causes: ocean conditions, hatchery genetics, harvest history.
theory_nodes.description: The most urgent ongoing Steelhead conservation emergency in Washington State.

cross_references.relationship: Coho and Steelhead share juvenile freshwater habitat (small streams). Stream restoration benefits both. 6PPD-quinone also affects Steelhead (less so than Coho).
Next Salmonidae species. Bull Trout = E=2 (near silence). Steelhead E=5 to Bull Trout E=2 = dramatic energy drop. Both char-family adjacent; very different ecology.

s36_pair.pairing_note: E=5 equilibrium. Metallic clean guitar (clean tone, bright reverb, Fender) + metallic splash clean jump. Both cross open water: Ventures crossed Pacific to Japan; Steelhead crosses ocean and returns. Metallic in sound, metallic in color.

milestones: First iteroparous species in Salmonidae chapter
Olympic Peninsula wild winter Steelhead crisis documented
